author: @frankbush333
text: The entertaining reality is the majority of the projects on this channel produce less then 5 watts of power and cost more then a 20 watt solar panel even on a cloudy day you would produce as much as 3/4 of these devices do. Producing something that was actually useful would be interesting to see... even a 50 watt 'rain panel' would be something. Don't get me wrong I enjoy the channel and am subscribed but it would be good to see projects that produce actual usable amounts of power beyond trickle charging a phone or powering an led.

author: @DavidPaulNewtonScott
text: A totally different thing I have just had a quote from an Australian company for 10 of their 1.2 Volt 20 amp hour batteries at 787 Australian dollars ie about £400 So that will give 240 watt hours enough to light my Portuguese off grid house and they will last at least 100 years. Job done all else is real time solar.

author: @johnbarry8185
text:
Rob, you REALLY ought to use a microwave turntable motor for something.  They are typically 6 RPMs, or less.  That means that you can get a HIGH voltage, for very few turns.  I placed one in the middle of a 5 gallon bucket lid, and used a screw through the lid, to turn it.
Keep up the good work.
Godspeed.

replies.author: @scurvofpcp

author: @johnbarry8185
text:
Okay Rob,
You HAVE to start a MAGNET company!  You show all these things that require DIFFERENT magnets, and we are buying them from somebody we DON'T KNOW.  THEY are getting rich, from US buying magnets, that YOU put into gizmos.  YOU should be making the money from the magnets that WE buy.  SO, start an online store for magnets, and we can buy them from YOU.  What would be great, is if you had a magnet 'kit', for each item that you make, and we can just say, "Hey Rob, will you please send me the magnet kit for...".  Keep up the good work.
Godspeed

author: @seckora

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: Sure. But that requires "right spring" , meaning , sufficient force to push the plunger back up , adequate dimensions for the contraption he made. Finding such a spring , defeats the purpose of what Rob is doing in these videos - giving ideas for further experimentation and/or research. He could , for an example , easily put four , or more motors/generators , to make more power , he also could make smaller cog (in a form of a cap of the motor) to multiply speed of rotation and so on...

author: @ThatTweedle
text:
this was AWESOME!!!! I used to love playing with those spinning tops when I was young.. and (for once) I don't have any ideas to make it easier LOL - I NEED to get a 3D printer!!! I have 2 or 3 old drills that are useless garbage right now and I would LOVE to just be tinkering away like this very day LOL
- I DID NOTICE that the serpentine coil was in a zig-zig (saw-tooth wave) pattern whereas the others you've shown were in square wave pattern... (I hope you know what I'm trying to describe, I don't know what else to call them lol) - what is the difference between them performance wise? AND is there a relationship between the density of the serpentine pattern and the size of the magnets and how close they are together? - I would assume more dense the waves in the coil and the more dense the magnets are, the more power you create, BUT the harder it would be to turn... 
- AND instead of having one row of magnets the size you use alternating N/S, what if you were to use smaller magnets and have two rows, alternating N/S... sort of like a zig-zag pattern that is closely spaced.... ???  oh that wouldn't work because then it would be all north on the top row and south on the bottom row... okay then some other pattern.... - I guess what I want to know is if you know if there has been experimentation done with different configurations instead of the fairly simple way we do it now? It would probably be early work by someone like Tesla, I just don't know who would have been the one to look into this stuff , So I don't know where to begin....
